title: Chicken Kiev
subtitle: Chicken breast stuffed with garlic herb butter, breaded and fried until crisp.
excerpt: Chicken Kiev is a chicken breast pounded thin, wrapped around a log of frozen garlic and parsley butter, coated in breadcrumbs, and fried until deep golden. When you cut it open, the butter runs out. The technique requires chilling time and care with the sealing, but the result is straightforward if you follow the sequence. Allow two hours total including the chilling.
sourceNotes: Chicken Kiev's origin is disputed between Ukrainian and French culinary traditions, though the preparation as a crumbed, butter-stuffed chicken breast became widely documented in the twentieth century under the name Kotleta po-kyivski in Soviet-era Ukrainian cooking. In the UK the dish became a mid-century restaurant and subsequently supermarket staple. The home-cooked version, made with good butter and properly chilled before frying, is substantially better than commercial versions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before frying, after crumbing, for up to three months. Cook from frozen in a 180°C oven for thirty-five to forty minutes, turning halfway.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Make the butter logs ahead and freeze separately.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The crumbed, stuffed Kievs can be refrigerated for up to 24 hours before frying. The garlic butter logs can be made and frozen a week ahead.
recipe.temperatureNote: For finishing in the oven after searing. Fan 160°C, gas mark 4.

recipeTools.notes: For pan-frying. Deep enough to fry in 2 cm of oil without spillage.

content.text: The key to chicken Kiev is keeping the butter inside. The butter is made first and frozen solid; the chicken is wrapped tightly around it and chilled again before coating; the sealed edge goes into the oil seam-side down first, setting it before the rest of the cooking. If you rush any of these stages, the butter escapes into the pan rather than pooling inside the finished Kiev.

The technique requires pounding the chicken flat. Use a large, heavy freezer bag to contain the pieces while you work, which keeps the flesh intact. The goal is an even thickness of about 5 mm — thin enough to wrap around the butter log and seal without too much bulk at the edges.

Shallow-frying in a finger of oil over medium-high heat, then finishing in a 180°C oven, gives a more reliably cooked result than deep-frying alone. The outside crisps in the pan; the centre finishes in the oven without risk of burning the crumbs.

Beat {{unsalted-butter}} of softened butter with the {{garlic}} grated garlic, the {{parsley-flat}} chopped parsley, the lemon zest, and a generous pinch of salt until combined. Spoon the butter onto a sheet of cling film and roll into a log about 12 cm long and 3 cm in diameter. Twist the ends and place in the freezer for at least thirty minutes until solid.
Place each {{chicken-breast}} chicken breast in a large freezer bag. Using a rolling pin, bash firmly until the breast is an even 5 mm thick all over. Season the inside surface with salt and pepper.

Cut the frozen butter log into four equal pieces. Place one piece on the edge of each flattened chicken breast. Roll the chicken around the butter tightly, folding in the sides as you go to form a sealed parcel. Place seam-side down on a plate, cover, and refrigerate for at least thirty minutes.

Set up three shallow bowls: seasoned {{plain-flour}}, beaten {{eggs}}, and {{breadcrumbs-dried}}. Working with one Kiev at a time, roll in flour and shake off the excess, then coat thoroughly in egg, then roll in breadcrumbs, pressing them on firmly. Return to the plate and refrigerate for a further thirty minutes.

Heat the oven to 180°C. Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a wide ovenproof frying pan over medium-high heat until shimmering. Place the Kievs seam-side down in the pan and fry for three minutes until golden. Turn carefully with tongs and fry for two minutes on each remaining side until deep golden all over. Transfer the pan to the oven and cook for fifteen minutes. Rest for five minutes before serving.

items.symptom: The butter has all leaked out during frying
items.cause: The butter was not frozen solid before wrapping, or the chicken was not sealed tightly enough.
items.fix: Make sure the butter log is completely solid before wrapping. Seal all edges of the chicken firmly, making sure there are no gaps. The seam-side-down start in the pan is important — it sets the seam before the butter warms enough to leak.

items.cause: The flour stage was skipped or insufficient — flour is what the egg adheres to.
items.fix: Flour first, then egg, then breadcrumbs, every time. Press the breadcrumbs on firmly. Chill after crumbing to set the coating.

items.symptom: The chicken is undercooked in the centre
items.cause: The breast was too thick after pounding, or the oven temperature was too low.
items.fix: Pound to an even 5 mm. Use an instant-read thermometer: 74°C at the centre means done. If unavailable, add five more minutes in the oven.

content.text: Chicken Kiev is claimed by both Ukraine and France, and the argument has been running since at least the mid-twentieth century. The Ukrainian version (kotleta po-kyivski) became a prestige dish in Soviet-era restaurants; the dish then travelled internationally through the Soviet diplomatic network and later through the post-war rise of restaurant dining. In the UK it became a Seventies dinner-party staple before becoming a supermarket frozen product. The frozen supermarket version bears little resemblance to one made with good butter and properly prepared. The home-cooked Kiev, with its golden crust and the butter pooled inside, is a different dish.
